elements.sent-2411-001: Determining the bubble wall velocity in first-order phase transitions is a challenging task, requiring the solution of (coupled) equations of motion for the scalar field and Boltzmann equations for the particles in the plasma.
elements.sent-2411-002: The collision terms appearing in the Boltzmann equation present a prominent source of uncertainty as they are often known only at leading log accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we derive upper and lower bounds on the wall velocity, corresponding to the local thermal equilibrium and ballistic limits.
elements.sent-2411-004: These bounds are completely independent of the collision terms.
elements.sent-2411-005: For the ballistic approximation, we argue that the inhomogeneous plasma temperature and velocity distributions across the bubble wall should be taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-006: This way, the hydrodynamic obstruction previously observed in local thermal equilibrium is also present for the ballistic approximation.
elements.sent-2411-007: This is essential for the ballistic approximation to provide a lower bound on the wall velocity.
elements.sent-2411-008: We use a model-independent approach to study the behaviour of the limiting wall velocities as a function of a few generic parameters, and we test our developments in the singlet extended Standard Model.
elements.sent-2411-009: First-order phase transitions (FOPTs) are common phenomena in daily life but are also naturally predicted to have occurred in the early Universe by many models beyond the Standard Model (SM).
elements.sent-2411-010: See, e.g., Refs.~\cite{Cline:1996mga,Carena:1997ki,Grojean:2004xa,Fromme:2006cm,Grojean:2006bp,Profumo:2007wc,Barger:2007im,Delaunay:2007wb,FileviezPerez:2008bj,Gil:2012ya,Dorsch:2013wja,Huang:2016odd,Jinno:2016knw,Chao:2017vrq,Beniwal:2017eik,Marzola:2017jzl,Kurup:2017dzf,Chen:2017cyc,Baldes:2018emh,Prokopec:2018tnq,Bian:2018bxr,Marzo:2018nov,Chala:2018opy,Zhou:2018zli,Alves:2018jsw,Azatov:2019png,DelleRose:2019pgi,VonHarling:2019rgb,Halverson:2020xpg,Ghosh:2020ipy,Huang:2020crf,DiBari:2021dri,Kierkla:2022odc,Morgante:2022zvc,Fujikura:2023fbi,Frandsen:2023vhu,Pasechnik:2023hwv,Feng:2024pab,Gao:2024pqm,Gao:2024fhm for a far from complete list of examples.}
elements.sent-2411-011: FOPTs are interesting not only because they can generate a stochastic background of gravitational waves (GWs)~\cite{Witten:1984rs,Hogan:1986dsh,Kosowsky:1992vn,Kosowsky:1992rz,Kamionkowski:1993fg , but also because they have far-reaching phenomenological applications.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Among other possibilities, they may be relevant for explaining the cosmic matter-antimatter asymmetry~\cite{Kuzmin:1985mm,Morrissey:2012db,Garbrecht:2018mrp,Cline:2020jre,Azatov:2021irb,Baldes:2021vyz,Huang:2022vkf,Chun:2023ezg,Cataldi:2024pgt or the generation of dark matter~\cite{Falkowski:2012fb,Baker:2019ndr,Chway:2019kft,Chao:2020adk,Azatov:2021ifm,Azatov:2022tii,Baldes:2022oev,Giudice:2024tcp,Azatov:2024crd,Ai:2024ikj .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Almost all the FOPT-related phenomena, as well as the generated GW signals, depend crucially on the bubble wall velocity $v_w$.
elements.sent-2411-014: For this reason, there have been a lot of works on bubble wall dynamics in recent years~\cite{BarrosoMancha:2020fay,Friedlander:2020tnq,Balaji:2020yrx,Cline:2021iff,Bea:2021zsu,Bigazzi:2021ucw,Ai:2021kak,Lewicki:2021pgr,Dorsch:2021nje,DeCurtis:2022hlx,Laurent:2022jrs,Wang:2022txy,Lewicki:2022nba,Janik:2022wsx,Li:2023xto,Ai:2023see,Krajewski:2023clt,Giombi:2023jqq,Wang:2023kux,Dorsch:2023tss,Sanchez-Garitaonandia:2023zqz,DeCurtis:2024hvh,Kang:2024xqk,Wang:2024wcs,Evans:2024ilx,Yuwen:2024hme, Ekstedt:2024fyq .}
elements.sent-2411-015: To determine $v_w$, one in principle needs to solve the coupled equation of motion (EoM) of the order-parameter scalar field and the Boltzmann equations for the particles that couple with the scalar field~\cite{Moore:1995si,Moore:1995ua .}
elements.sent-2411-016: See Refs.~\cite{Liu:1992tn,Dorsch:2018pat,Wang:2020zlf,Laurent:2020gpg,Laurent:2022jrs,Jiang:2022btc, Ekstedt:2024fyq for some studies following this approach.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In~\cite{Ignatius:1993qn,Heckler:1994uu,Kurki-Suonio:1996gkq,Espinosa:2010hh,Huber:2011aa,Huber:2013kj the backreaction of the fluid onto the expanding bubbles is incorporated by adding an effective friction term to the scalar field EoM.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Solving the Boltzmann equations and the scalar EoM is however challenging not only because they are coupled integrodifferential equations, but also because the computation of the collision terms suffers from large uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-019: It is therefore highly desirable to derive the upper and lower bounds of the wall velocity with simpler approaches, which is the goal of the present work.
elements.sent-2411-020: Such upper and lower bounds can be very useful in the initial exploration of a BSM model.
elements.sent-2411-021: For example, certain dark matter generation mechanisms require bubbles with very slow walls \cite{Baker:2019ndr, Chway:2019kft .}
elements.sent-2411-022: To find out whether a specific BSM realization can give successful dark matter generation, one might first determine the upper and lower bound for the wall velocity.
elements.sent-2411-023: These results can then motivate a more detailed computation of $v_w$, including a solution of the Boltzmann equations (or save one from wasting time and numerical resources).
elements.sent-2411-024: Our estimates for the upper and lower bound of the wall velocity correspond to the two limiting cases for the deviation from equilibrium of the particles in the plasma.
elements.sent-2411-025: Intuitively, the larger the deviation from thermal equilibrium of the plasma, the greater the friction the bubble wall experiences.
elements.sent-2411-026: One can imagine (and we will demonstrate in this work) that the deviation from equilibrium is a monotonically decreasing function of the collision rate $\Gamma$ for the particles in the plasma.
elements.sent-2411-027: Let us assume that the interaction strength is infinitely strong, i.e., $\Gamma\rightarrow\infty$, particles can instantaneously relax to their equilibrium state after being perturbed, and therefore, the plasma would remain in local thermal equilibrium as the wall passes through.
elements.sent-2411-028: This is the local thermal equilibrium (LTE) approximation~\cite{Balaji:2020yrx,Ai:2021kak,Ai:2023see , which can provide the lower bound of the friction experienced by the wall and the upper bound of the velocity.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Although in LTE there is no {\it dissipative friction, there is still a backreaction force due to hydrodynamic effects~\cite{Ignatius:1993qn,Konstandin:2010dm , which could lead to a stationary motion of the wall.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Such hydrodynamic effects are caused by the inhomogeneous temperature and velocity distributions near the wall.
elements.sent-2411-031: It was noted in Ref.~\cite{Ai:2021kak that in LTE, the wall velocity can be determined very simply and efficiently with a new matching condition in the hydrodynamic quantities across the bubble wall due to the conservation of entropy.}
elements.sent-2411-032: This method has been more thoroughly studied in Ref.~\cite{Ai:2023see .}
elements.sent-2411-033: In the opposite limit, $\Gamma\rightarrow 0$, particles do not collide with each other, and the friction can be studied by analyzing particle transmission across the wall.
elements.sent-2411-034: This is the {\it ballistic approximation that is usually used for ultra-relativistic bubble walls~\cite{Bodeker:2009qy,Bodeker:2017cim,Hoche:2020ysm,Azatov:2020ufh,Gouttenoire:2021kjv,GarciaGarcia:2022yqb,Ai:2023suz , but has also been applied for slow walls in Refs.~\cite{Liu:1992tn,BarrosoMancha:2020fay,Wang:2024wcs .}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: In contrast to the LTE case, the inhomogeneity in the plasma temperature and velocity distributions is usually not considered in the ballistic approximation.\footnote{An exception is the recent work~\cite{Wang:2024wcs which considers the inhomogeneous distribution in the temperature but not in the fluid velocity.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Without considering it, the crucial hydrodynamic effects are ignored and the estimate of the velocity could be misleading.
elements.sent-2411-037: In particular, it has been observed when solving the scalar EoM and Boltzmann equations~\cite{Cline:2021iff,Laurent:2022jrs (see also Refs.~\cite{DeCurtis:2022hlx,DeCurtis:2023hil ), but also in a pure LTE analysis~\cite{Ai:2023see,Ai:2024shx that the frictional pressure has a peak at the Jouguet velocity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Such a peak is important as it tells us that the friction is not a monotonous function of the wall velocity and thus one cannot simply use the asymptotic value of the friction in the ultrarelativistic limit~\cite{Bodeker:2009qy to determine whether a bubble wall runs away or not~\cite{Ai:2024shx .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In this work, we carefully account for the mentioned inhomogeneity in the plasma temperature and velocity across the wall.
elements.sent-2411-040: As a result, we observe a similar pressure curve peaked at the Jouguet velocity for the ballistic approximation, and we can extract a lower bound on the wall velocity.
elements.sent-2411-041: The goal of this work is three-fold:.
elements.sent-2411-042: We update the ballistic approximation to account for the inhomogeneous temperature and velocity profile, promoting it such that it gives us a lower bound on $v_w$.
elements.sent-2411-043: We demonstrate that the ballistic approximation gives the upper bound of the friction (equivalently lower bound of the wall velocity), and the LTE approximation the lower bound.
elements.sent-2411-044: We demonstrate in practice how the bounds on the velocity can be applied to simple and realistic models.
elements.sent-2411-045: In this paper, we will see a few different length scales for the phase transition (PT) dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-046: Here we summarise them in Table~\ref{tab:scales .}
elements.sent-2411-047: The reader may refer to it when these length scales are introduced.

elements.sent-2411-048: The remainder of this article is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-049: In the next section, we give a brief review of the general aspects of bubble wall dynamics and hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-050: In Sec.~\ref{sec:Bounds we discuss the two approximations used in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-051: We derive the ballistic distribution functions with hydrodynamics correctly integrated and derive a new effective matching condition for the ballistic approximation.
elements.sent-2411-052: In Sec.~\ref{sec:proof , we argue that the LTE and ballistic limits establish bounds on the wall friction and velocity.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In Sec.~\ref{sec:numerics , we present our numerical results both for a model-independent analysis and for an example model.}
elements.sent-2411-055: For completeness, some technical details are left in appendices.

elements.section-2411-002: A brief review of bubble wall dynamics and hydrodynamics
elements.sent-2411-057: We illustrate the bubble wall dynamics in Fig.~\ref{fig:bubble_wall_dynamics , taking a deflagration as an example.}
elements.sent-2411-058: The relevant quantities for a hydrodynamic description are the fluid temperature and velocity distributions, $T(\xi)$ and $v(\xi)$, where $\xi=r/t$ with $r$ the radial coordinate and $t$ the time since nucleation.
elements.sent-2411-059: Since there is no other scale in the problem, the solutions for $v$ and $T$ are self-similar, i.e., they only depend on $\xi$.
elements.sent-2411-060: The bubble wall and the shock front can be viewed as having zero size at the hydrodynamics scale.
elements.sent-2411-061: The quantities on both sides of these fronts (either the phase front, i.e. bubble wall, or shock front) are therefore related by matching conditions.
elements.sent-2411-062: To study the matching conditions at the wall and to determine the wall velocity, one needs to zoom in on the fronts to a scale where the wall has a finite width.
elements.sent-2411-063: One uses the coupled EoM between the scalar field and the plasma to find the matching conditions.
elements.sent-2411-064: There are two standard (hydrodynamic) matching conditions given by the conservation of the total energy-momentum tensor.
elements.sent-2411-065: For both of the two approximations adopted in this work, there is another matching condition with which one can fully determine the system for a given nucleation temperature $T_n$.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{Figs/bubble_wall_dynamics.pdf} \caption{Sketch of bubble wall dynamics using the deflagration mode as an example. The bubble wall and shock front are viewed as having zero size at the hydrodynamic scale. The hydrodynamic quantities $T$ and $v$ are discontinuous at these fronts. To study the matching conditions, one then needs to zoom in on these fronts. } \label{fig:bubble_wall_dynamics}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Coupled scalar and plasma equations of motion
elements.sent-2411-066: The analysis of the friction on bubble walls is usually based on the following coupled EoMs for the background field and plasma~\cite{Liu:1992tn, Moore:1995ua,Moore:1995si , \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\Box\phi+\frac{\d V(\phi)}{\d\phi}+\sum_i\frac{\d m^2_i(\phi)}{\d\phi}\int \frac{\d^3{\bf p}}{(2\pi)^32E_i}\,f_i(p,x)=0\,,\label{eq:EoM-bubble} \frac{\d f_i}{\d t}=-\C[f]\,, \label{eq:Boltz-Eq}\end{equation} \end{subequations where $\Box=\partial_\mu \partial^\mu$, $f_i(p,x)$ are the particle distribution functions, and $E_i=\sqrt{\vec{p ^2_i+m_i^2}$ the particle energies.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Considering a bubble wall expanding in the $z$-direction, and working in the rest frame of the wall where the wall position is taken as $z=0$, one can obtain the driving and frictional pressures (see, e.g., Ref.~\cite{Ai:2024shx ) \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\P_{\rm driving}=V(\phi_s)-V(\phi_b)\equiv \Delta V\,, \label{eq:Pdriving} \P_{\rm friction}= - \int_{-\delta}^{\delta} \d z\, (\partial_z\phi)\left(\sum_i\frac{\d m^2_i(\phi)}{\d\phi}\int \frac{\d^3{\bf p}}{(2\pi)^32E_i}\,f_i(p,z)\right)\,,\label{eq:Pfriction}\end{equation} \end{subequations where $\phi_{s,b}$ are the field values at the symmetric and symmetry-broken minima, respectively.\footnote{Although generally, FOPT does not necessarily involve symmetry breaking, we will use the terminology of a symmetry-breaking FOPT throughout.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Above, $\delta \gg L_w$ is a length scale.
elements.sent-2411-069: Its value should be chosen such that $|\partial_z\phi/(\Delta\phi)^2|_{\pm \delta}\ll 1$, where $\Delta\phi=\phi_b-\phi_s$.
elements.sent-2411-070: For a wall described by a hyperbolic tangent, one may take, e.g., $\delta= \mathcal O(5 L_w) $.
elements.sent-2411-071: It can be effectively understood to be infinity in the context of the scalar field EoM.
elements.sent-2411-072: One can actually use conservation of total energy-momentum to write the frictional pressure in a different way.

elements.sent-2411-082: If the interactions among the particles are very efficient, the plasma can remain close to thermal equilibrium during the bubble expansion.\footnote{Whether the plasma can remain close to LTE also depends on the strength of the force imposed from the wall on the particles.}

elements.sent-2411-086: On the other hand, if the interactions among the particles are extremely weak, one can take the collision term to be zero in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Boltz-Eq and particles in the plasma do not collide with each other while passing through the wall.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Again, the Boltzmann equations become very simple and solutions can be obtained straightforwardly.
elements.sent-2411-089: These two approximations dramatically simplify the problem but meanwhile, provide the lower and upper bounds on the friction and hence are still very important.
elements.sent-2411-090: Mathematically, the conditions for the LTE and ballistic approximation to be valid are \begin{subequations \begin{equation}L_w/\gamma_w \gg L_{\rm MFP} \propto \frac{1}{\Gamma} \,,\qquad (LTE condition) \label{eq:ballistic_condition} L_w/\gamma_w \ll L_{\rm MFP} \propto \frac{1}{\Gamma} \,,\qquad (ballistic condition)\end{equation} \end{subequations where $L_w$ is the wall width {\it in the wall rest frame , $L_{\rm MFP}$ is the mean free path {\it in the plasma frame , $\Gamma$ characterizes the collision rate between the particles, and $\gamma_w\equiv 1/\sqrt{1-v_w^2}$ is the Lorentz boost factor corresponding to $v_w$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-095: There are three ways to apply the ballistic approximation.
elements.sent-2411-096: The first way is commonly considered in the literature~\cite{Bodeker:2009qy,Bodeker:2017cim,Hoche:2020ysm,Azatov:2020ufh,Gouttenoire:2021kjv,GarciaGarcia:2022yqb,Ai:2023suz ; one fixes the collision strength and hence $L_{\rm MFP}$ to be the one determined by the model under study, and condition~\eqref{eq:ballistic_condition is satisfied for sufficiently large $\gamma_w$.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Therefore, the ballistic approximation is used for ultrarelativistic bubble walls, i.e. in the large $\gamma_w$ limit.
elements.sent-2411-098: This is a faithful application of this approximation, but it only tells us the friction in the ultrarelativistic regime.
elements.sent-2411-099: In the other two approaches, one takes the limit $\Gamma\rightarrow 0$ for given $\gamma_w$.
elements.sent-2411-100: In this way, one does not faithfully derive the wall velocity for the considered model but uses the ballistic approximation to derive the upper bound of the friction and thus the lower bound of the wall velocity (the same holds for the use the LTE approximation in this work).
elements.sent-2411-101: In the approach of~\cite{Liu:1992tn,BarrosoMancha:2020fay , the plasma temperature and velocity were taken as constant.}
elements.sent-2411-102: In this work, on the other hand we also consider inhomogeneous plasma temperature and velocity distributions, thus accounting for hydrodynamic obstruction~\cite{Konstandin:2010dm,Cline:2021iff,Laurent:2022jrs,Ai:2023see,Ai:2024shx .}
elements.sent-2411-103: We shall observe a higher pressure barrier in the ballistic approximation than in the LTE approximation.

elements.subsection-2411-002: The standard matching conditions and fluid equations
elements.sent-2411-105: To study the matching conditions for the hydrodynamic quantities across the wall, it is convenient to work in the rest frame of the bubble wall (see the right panel of Fig.~\ref{fig:bubble_wall_dynamics ).}
elements.sent-2411-106: The matching conditions are usually formulated in terms of the energy density and pressure with the tree-level scalar potential energy absorbed: $e=e_f+V(\phi)$, $p=p_f-V(\phi) \equiv -V_{\rm eff (\phi,T)$.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Note that the fluid enthalpy is $\omega=e_f+p_f=e+p$.
elements.sent-2411-108: From energy-momentum conservation, we have in equilibrium \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\omega(z)\gamma^2(z) v(z)={\rm const}\, , \label{eq:em-con-2} \omega(z)\gamma^2(z) v^2(z)+\frac{1}{2}(\partial_z\phi(z))^2+p(z)={\rm const}\, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations where $v >0$ is defined through $u^\mu=\gamma(1,0,0,-v)$ and $\gamma(v)=1/\sqrt{1-v^2}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: To get the matching conditions, we take $z=\pm \delta'$, (c.f.. Table~\ref{tab:scales ).}
elements.sent-2411-110: Since the above equations are valid only for LTE (due to the use of the perfect fluid energy-momentum tensor), $\delta'$ has to be chosen to be much larger than the mean free path in the wall frame $\gamma_w L_{\rm MFP}$ (which should really be understood as the mean free path of the particle with the smallest interaction rate).
elements.sent-2411-111: This way, all particles relax to equilibrium at $\pm \delta'$.
elements.sent-2411-112: On the other hand, $\delta'$ should be much smaller than the macroscopic scale so that it can be essentially viewed as infinitesimally thin at the hydrodynamics scale.
elements.sent-2411-113: Therefore, we have $\gamma_w R_{\rm bubble \gg\delta' \gg \gamma_w L_{\rm MFP}$.}
elements.sent-2411-114: Then we obtain the following two well-known matching conditions \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\omega_+\gamma_+^2v_+=\omega_-\gamma_-^2v_-\, ,\label{eq:conditionA} \omega_+\gamma_+^2v_+^2+p_+=\omega_-\gamma_-^2v_-^2+p_-\, ,\label{eq:conditionB}\end{equation} \end{subequations where a subscript ``$\pm$'' is used to denote quantities in front of/behind the bubble wall.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: To be clear, $e_+=e_s(T_+)$, $e_-=e_b(T_-)$ and similarly for $p_{\pm }$.

elements.section-2411-004: Demonstration that LTE and ballistic provide bounds on the wall velocity
elements.sent-2411-192: Having discussed how the LTE and ballistic approximations simplify the computation of the bubble wall velocity, here we demonstrate that they indeed provide bounds on the wall velocity and friction.
elements.sent-2411-193: We will first prove that the LTE gives an upper bound of the wall velocity using the template model (cf.
elements.sent-2411-195: Then, we give a more general argument based on the linearized Boltzmann equation.

elements.sent-2411-214: The general Boltzmann equation for the deviation from equilibrium $\delta f=f-f^{\rm eq}$ of a single particle can be written as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Boltzmann} \left(p^z\partial_z-\frac{1}{2}\partial_z(m^2)\partial_{p^z}\right) \delta f+\lambdaC[\delta f](z,p^z) = -\left(p^z\partial_z-\frac{1}{2}\partial_z(m^2)\partial_{p^z}\right) f^{\rm eq} \equiv S(z,p^z)\,,\end{equation} where the parameter $\lambda$ represents the strength of the interactions involved in the collisions.\footnote{Note that $\lambda$ does not correspond to a physical quantity and should be set to 1 in a calculation.}
elements.sent-2411-215: We introduce it here to study the variation of the solution with respect to the collision strength and to take the different limits.
elements.sent-2411-216: The ballistic limit can be obtained by taking $\lambda\to0$, while LTE is obtained in the limit $\lambda\to\infty$.

elements.section-2411-005: Comparison between LTE and ballistic results
elements.sent-2411-259: As the LTE and ballistic approximations respectively offer an upper and lower bound on the true wall velocity, it can be useful to compare these two approximations to find constraints on the wall velocity without having to solve a complicated system of Boltzmann equations and scalar EoM.
elements.sent-2411-260: Not only are these equations challenging and computationally expensive to solve, they also suffer from large theoretical uncertainties coming from the collision rates $\Gamma$ that appear in the Boltzmann equations.
elements.sent-2411-261: These collision rates are typically computed to leading-log accuracy, which is rather imprecise and can lead to errors of $\mathcal{O (1)$ \cite{Moore:1995si,Cline:2021iff,Laurent:2022jrs,Kozaczuk:2015owa .}}
elements.sent-2411-262: The LTE and ballistic approximations allow us to avoid this problem since they correspond to the limits $\Gamma\to\infty$ and $\Gamma\to 0$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-263: Thus, even if $\Gamma$ is completely unknown, one can still obtain useful information on $v_w$ with these approximations in the form of an interval in which it must reside.
elements.sent-2411-264: Unfortunately, making a general comparison between LTE and ballistic is not a straightforward task as these two approximations depend on a different set of variables.
elements.sent-2411-265: In general, this mapping from one approximation to the other cannot be made in a model-independent way.
elements.sent-2411-266: It either necessitates specifying a model, in which case the mapping can be done exactly, or making approximations which allows one to make more general conclusions that should still hold at least qualitatively.
elements.sent-2411-267: We will explore both options in the following.
elements.sent-2411-268: To obtain a more general and qualitative understanding of how the LTE and ballistic approximation compare to one another, we now study a set of simplified models.
elements.sent-2411-269: We will assume that the plasma contains ${\tilde{g _{\star }$ massless degrees of freedom and ${g_{*,\phi }$ degrees of freedom with a mass of $m_+$ and $m_-$ outside and inside the bubble, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-270: The pressure in this model can therefore be expressed as \begin{equation}\label{eq:EOS} p_{s/b}(T) = \frac{\pi^2 {\tilde{g}_{\star}}}{90}T^4 + {g_{\star,\phi}}\, p_{M,s/b}(T) - \epsilon_\pm\,,\end{equation} where $\epsilon_\pm$ is the vacuum contribution (which is approximated to be independent of $T$), and $p_{M,s/b}$ is the thermal pressure of a single degree of freedom with mass $m_\pm$.
elements.sent-2411-271: To simplify the discussion, we will assume that $p_{M,s/b}$ is given by the one-loop Maxwell-Boltzmann pressure, which is given by \begin{equation}p_{M,s/b}(T) =\frac{\pi^4}{90} \frac{m_\pm^2 T^2}{2\pi^2} K_2\left(\frac{m_\pm}{T}\right)\,,\end{equation} where $K_n(x)$ is the modified Bessel function of the second kind.
elements.sent-2411-272: Above, we have added by hand an extra factor $\pi^4/90\approx 1.08$ so that $p_M$ reproduces the value of the Bose-Einstein distribution for $m_{\pm =0$.}
elements.sent-2411-273: This way, $g_{\star,\phi}$ can be taken to be the effective number of degrees of freedom coupling with the scalar $\phi$, \begin{equation}{g_{\star,\phi}}=\sum_{i_B}g_{i_B,\phi}+\sum_{i_F} \frac{7}{8} g_{i_F,\phi} \,,\end{equation} where $g_{i_B,\phi}$ is the number of internal degrees of freedom of boson $i_B$, and $g_{i_F,\phi}$ of fermion $i_F$.
elements.sent-2411-274: It follows directly that the enthalpy and energy densities are given by \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\omega_{s/b}(T) = \frac{2\pi^2 {\tilde{g}_\star}}{45}T^4+{g_{\star,\phi}}\frac{\pi^4}{90}\frac{m_\pm^3 T}{2\pi^2}K_3\left(\frac{m_\pm}{T}\right)\,, e_{s/b}(T) = \frac{\pi^2 {\tilde{g}_\star}}{30}T^4 + {g_{\star,\phi}}{\frac{\pi^4}{90}}\frac{m_\pm^2 T}{2\pi^2}\left[ m_\pm K_3\left(\frac{m_\pm}{T}\right) - T K_2\left(\frac{m_\pm}{T}\right)\right] + \epsilon_\pm\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-051: \begin{equation}\omega_{s/b}(T) = \frac{2\pi^2 {\tilde{g}_\star}}{45}T^4+{g_{\star,\phi}}\frac{\pi^4}{90}\frac{m_\pm^3 T}{2\pi^2}K_3\left(\frac{m_\pm}{T}\right)\,, e_{s/b}(T) = \frac{\pi^2 {\tilde{g}_\star}}{30}T^4 + {g_{\star,\phi}}{\frac{\pi^4}{90}}\frac{m_\pm^2 T}{2\pi^2}\left[ m_\pm K_3\left(\frac{m_\pm}{T}\right) - T K_2\left(\frac{m_\pm}{T}\right)\right] + \epsilon_\pm\,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-275: However, one can see that the variables ${\tilde{g _\star}$, ${g_{\star,\phi }$ and $\epsilon_\pm$ only appear in the combinations \begin{equation}b = \frac{{g_{\star,\phi}}}{{\tilde{g}_\star}}\,,\qquad\qquad \epsilon = \frac{\epsilon_+ - \epsilon_-}{{\tilde{g}_\star}},\end{equation} in all the matching equations.}}
elements.sent-2411-276: This effectively lowers the number of independent variables in this model to four.
elements.sent-2411-277: Finally, to simplify the LTE calculation, one can map the EoS \eqref{eq:EOS to the template model (see Appendix \ref{app:template ) by computing the thermodynamic quantities $\alpha(T_n)$, $c_{s/b (T_n)$ and $\Psi(T_n)$ with Eqs.~(\ref{eq:alpha ), (\ref{eq:thermo-quantities-from-p ) and (\ref{eq:Psi ).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-278: This typically gives a reasonable approximation and allows one to use the method presented in Ref.~\cite{Ai:2023see to compute $v_w$.}
elements.sent-2411-279: However, to guarantee accurate results, we will keep the full EoS \eqref{eq:EOS in what follows and use the {\tt WallGo package \cite{Ekstedt:2024fyq to find the LTE wall velocity (based on hydrodynamics \cite{Ai:2021kak ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-280: We now move to comparing the wall velocity obtained within this model using the ballistic and LTE limits.
elements.sent-2411-281: As argued previously, these limits give respectively a lower and upper bound on the possible wall velocities and can therefore contain helpful information.
elements.sent-2411-282: We will be particularly interested in the difference between these two predictions.
elements.sent-2411-283: If the difference is large (or if they predict different types of solutions), the true wall velocity remains poorly constrained and it is required to solve a set of Boltzmann equations to obtain a better estimate.
elements.sent-2411-284: On the other hand, if the difference is small, the collision operator has very little effect on the wall velocity and it may be unnecessary to solve the Boltzmann equations.
elements.sent-2411-285: In what follows, it will be convenient to describe the strength of the PT with a slightly different definition of $\alpha$ than the one defined in terms of the pseudo trace $\bar\theta$ (see Eq.\ (\ref{eq:alpha )).}
elements.sent-2411-286: Instead, we will use a definition based on the pressure difference: \begin{equation}\label{eq:alphap} \alpha_p(T) = -(1+1/c_b^2)\left(\frac{p_s(T)-p_b(T)}{3\omega_s(T)}\right) = \alpha(T) - \frac{1-\Psi(T)}{3}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-287: The two definitions are approximately equal for strong PTs, but slightly disagree for weaker ones.
elements.sent-2411-288: In particular, by definition, $\alpha_p$ vanishes when $\Delta V=0$, so it can always take values from 0 to $\infty$, while $\alpha$ cannot be smaller than $(1-\Psi)/3$.
elements.sent-2411-289: Furthermore, the wall velocity always goes to 0 in the limit $\alpha_p\to 0$.
elements.sent-2411-290: These properties make it easier to interpret and present results expressed in terms of $\alpha_p$ rather than $\alpha$ (one can of course always go easily from one to the other if needed using Eq.\ (\ref{eq:alphap )).}

elements.subsection-2411-008: Example model: the Standard Model coupled to a gauge singlet
elements.sent-2411-410: Let us now put our approximations to the test in a concrete example model.
elements.sent-2411-411: We choose the $\mathbb Z_2$-symmetric xSM, where the Standard Model Higgs $h$ is coupled to a new gauge singlet $s$ (see e.g. Refs.~\cite{Espinosa:1993bs, Barger:2007im, Espinosa:2011ax, Kozaczuk:2015owa, Laurent:2022jrs ), with tree level potential \begin{equation}V(h,s)=-\frac{\mu_h^2}{2}h^2+\frac{\lambda_h}{4}h^4+\frac{1}{2}\left(m_s^2-\frac{\lambda_{hs}v^2}{2}\right)s^2+\frac{\lambda_s}{4}s^4+\frac{\lambda_{hs}}{4}h^2 s^2\,,\end{equation} where $v=\mu_h/\sqrt{\lambda_h =246\,{\rm GeV}$ is the VEV of the SM Higgs $h$.}}
elements.sent-2411-412: We consider a region of parameter space where the PT proceeds in two steps: first, the singlet obtains a VEV, and in the second step, the Higgs obtains a VEV and the singlet goes back to zero.
elements.sent-2411-413: We use Benchmark 1 defined in Ref.~\cite{Ekstedt:2024fyq , with $m_s = 120 \, {\rm GeV}$, $\lambda_{hs}$ and $\lambda_s = 1$ and focus on the second step of the PT for two choices of the nucleation temperature $T_n = 90, 100 \, {\rm GeV}$.}

elements.sent-2411-439: As a result, it overestimates the wall velocity, and finds a detonation solution rather than a hybrid.
elements.sent-2411-440: The LTE and ballistic approximations are two approaches for estimating bubble wall velocities in FOPTs.
elements.sent-2411-441: These approximations are typically applicable within specific regions of the parameter space and wall velocities.
elements.sent-2411-442: For instance, the LTE approximation is generally reliable for relatively low wall velocities ($\gamma_w\lsim \O(10)$) {\it and strong interactions among plasma particles.}
elements.sent-2411-443: In contrast, the ballistic approximation is suitable in scenarios with either very weak interactions or very high wall velocities (i.e., when $\gamma_w \gg 1$).
elements.sent-2411-444: In this work, however, we use these approximations to derive general bounds on friction and wall velocity.
elements.sent-2411-445: The two limits correspond to extreme collision rates: $\Gamma \rightarrow 0$ for the ballistic regime and $\Gamma \rightarrow \infty$ for LTE.
elements.sent-2411-446: By linearising the general Boltzmann equation, we have analytically demonstrated that the LTE and ballistic limits indeed establish upper and lower bounds on the wall velocity, respectively, so that $v_w \in [v_w^{\rm b , v_w^{\rm LTE ]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-447: As a key development in this work, we have demonstrated how hydrodynamics effects can be incorporated into the ballistic approximation.
elements.sent-2411-448: To achieve this, we account for inhomogeneities in the fluid temperature and velocity while solving the Liouville equation, i.e., the collisionless Boltzmann equation.
elements.sent-2411-449: The resulting general solution depends on $\{T_\pm, v_\pm\}$, where $T_\pm$ and $v_\pm$ denote the fluid temperature and velocity in front of/behind the bubble wall, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-450: From this solution for the particle distribution functions, we compute the friction on the wall, $\P_{\rm friction ^{\rm b (T_+, T_-, v_+, v_-)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-451: We have further shown that $\P_{\rm friction ^{\rm b =\P_{\rm driving}\equiv \Delta V$ can serve as an additional matching condition, enabling us to fully determine the wall velocity.}}
elements.sent-2411-452: With hydrodynamics correctly integrated, we demonstrate that the ballistic pressure exhibits a non-monotonic behavior, featuring a local peak at the Jouguet velocity.
elements.sent-2411-453: This finding aligns with previous observations made using the LTE approximation or the complete solution of the Boltzmann equation~\cite{Cline:2021iff, Laurent:2022jrs, DeCurtis:2022hlx, DeCurtis:2023hil, Ai:2024shx .}
elements.sent-2411-454: We have conducted an extensive scan for the wall velocities obtained in the two approximations in a model-independent framework.
elements.sent-2411-455: We identify parameter regions with a small difference in the two solutions ($v^{\rm LTE _w- v_w^{\rm b}$), where solving the full Boltzmann equation is not necessary, and those with a relatively large difference.}
elements.sent-2411-456: For deflagration/hybrid motions, we have observed that in some part of parameter space, there are ballistic solutions but no LTE ones.
elements.sent-2411-457: This is reasonable as the ballistic pressure is always larger than the LTE pressure so there are situations in which the ballistic pressure can beat the driving pressure before reaching the Jouguet velocity while the LTE pressure cannot.
elements.sent-2411-458: While it has been shown in the previous work~\cite{Ai:2023see that there is no stable detonation solution in LTE, we have shown that this is not the case for the ballistic approximation, although the parameter space allowing for such solutions is relatively narrow.}
elements.sent-2411-459: For the ballistic approximation, we also provide a detailed analysis of cases where particles become very massive after entering the bubble, as occurs in models with conformal symmetry, for example.
elements.sent-2411-460: In these instances, we offer either an analytic solution for the wall velocity in the detonation case or a numerical fit for deflagration and hybrid cases.
elements.sent-2411-461: Finally, we have applied our methods to a specific model: the Standard Model coupled to a gauge singlet, and obtained the wall velocities in different limits, comparing them with the one obtained from solving the full Boltzmann equation using the package {\tt WallGo recently released in Ref.~\cite{Ekstedt:2024fyq .}}
elements.sent-2411-462: For the benchmark point we considered, we have seen that the velocity obtained by solving the full Boltzmann equation does lie between the LTE and ballistic bounds.

elements.section-2411-007: Model-independent hydrodynamics with the template EoS
elements.sent-2411-473: To have a model-independent analysis, one usually introduces an EoS for the plasma.
elements.sent-2411-474: A good EoS is the so-called template model~\cite{Leitao:2014pda that has been used in various studies~\cite{Giese:2020rtr,Ai:2023see,Sanchez-Garitaonandia:2023zqz ).}}

elements.sent-2411-478: To formulate the matching conditions in terms of model-independent parameters, we introduce the $D$ operator through~\cite{Giese:2020rtr \begin{equation}\Delta p=p_s(T_+) - p_b(T_-) =\left[p_s(T_+)-p_b(T_+)\right]+\left[p_b(T_+)-p_b(T_-)\right]\notag \equiv Dp (T_+)+\delta p(T_+,T_-)\,,\end{equation} and similarly for the energy density and enthalpy.}
elements.sent-2411-479: Above we have defined $Dp(T)\equiv p_s(T)-p_b(T)$, the difference of a thermodynamic quantity in the two phases but at the same temperature.
elements.sent-2411-480: A quantity that turns out to be of particular importance is \begin{equation}\frac{\delta p(T_+,T_-)}{\delta \rho(T_+,T_-)}=\frac{p_b(T_+)-p_b(T_-)}{e_b(T_+)-e_b(T_-)}\equiv \zeta^2(T_+,T_-)\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-481: In the template EoS, we have $\zeta(T_+,T_-)=c_b$ which is constant.
elements.sent-2411-482: Ref.~\cite{Giese:2020rtr, Giese:2020znk then introduces the following quantity for the PT strength \begin{equation}\label{eq:alpha} \alpha(T)=\frac{D\Bar{\theta}}{3\omega_s(T)}\,, \qquad \Bar{\theta}=\left(e-\frac{p}{c_b^2}\right)\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-483: With the parameter $\alpha_+\equiv \alpha(T_+)$, one can write the matching conditions as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{eq:match0} \frac{\Delta p}{3\omega_+}\left(1-\frac{v_+ v_-}{\zeta^2}\right)=v_+v_-\alpha_+\,, \label{eq:match1} \frac{v_+}{v_-}=\frac{\left(\frac{v_+v_-}{\zeta^2}-1\right)+3\alpha_+}{\left(\frac{v_+v_-}{\zeta^2}-1\right)+3v_+v_-\alpha_+}\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-484: For detonations, $T_+$ and $v_+$ are equal to the nucleation temperature $T_n$ and $v_w$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-485: For deflagrations/hybrids, $T_+$ can be related to $T_n$ through the fluid equations between the wall and the shock front, while there is an additional condition either through $v_-=v_w$, or $v_-=c_b$.

elements.sent-2411-490: As $T_+$ is related to $T_n$, either directly or through the fluid equations and matching conditions at the shock front, the system of equations is closed and one can determine the wall velocity for a given $T_n$.
elements.sent-2411-491: More specifically, for detonations, $T_+=T_n$ and therefore $\alpha_+=\alpha_n\equiv \alpha(T_n)$, $\Psi_+=\Psi_n\equiv \Psi(T_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-493: In conclusion, within the LTE approximation and the template model, the dynamics can be fully determined by four thermodynamic quantities: $\alpha_n$, $\Psi_n$, $c_s$ and $c_b$.

elements.sent-2411-595: We then get $\rho(z';z), \rho(z'';z) \to |p^z|$ and \begin{equation}f_-^{\tau\to0}(p^z,z;\vecp_\perp) = -\Theta(-p^z)\,\frac{C(p^z,z;\vecp_\perp)}{\tau p^z}\,f^{\rm eq}(p^z,z;\vecp_\perp)\int_z^\infty \d z'\exp\left[\frac{C(p^z,z;\vecp_\perp)}{\tau p^z}\,(z'-z)\right]\notag =\Theta(-p^z) f^{\rm eq}(p^z,z;\vecp_\perp)\int^0_{-\infty} \d x\, \e^x = \Theta(-p^z)f^{\rm eq}(p^z,z;\vecp_\perp)\,.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-598: For $|z|<\Lambda$, one can then separate the integral into two parts: \begin{equation}f_-^{\tau\to\infty}(p^z,z;\vecp_\perp) = \frac{\Theta(-p^z)}{\tau}\int_z^\Lambda \d z'\, \frac{C[-\rho(z';z),z';\vecp_\perp]}{\rho(z';z)}\,f^{\rm eq}[-\rho(z';z),z';\vecp_\perp]\exp(0)\notag +\frac{\Theta(-p^z)}{\tau}\frac{C[-\rho(\infty;z),\infty;\vecp_\perp]}{\rho(\infty;z)}f^{\rm eq}[-\rho(\infty;z),\infty;\vecp_\perp]\int_\Lambda^\infty \d z' \exp\left[-\frac{C[-\rho(\infty;z),\infty;\vecp_\perp]}{\tau\rho(\infty;z)}(z'-z)\right]\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-599: The second integral can be done directly and gives to first order in $\Lambda/\tau$ \begin{equation}f_-^{\tau\to\infty}(p^z,z;\vecp_\perp) = f^{\rm eq}[-\rho(\infty;z),\infty;\vecp_\perp] = f^{t_+}(p^z,z;\vecp_\perp)\,,\end{equation} where recall again that $f^{t_+}$ is defined for modes with $p^z<-\sqrt{m_-^2-m(z)^2}$.
elements.sent-2411-600: One can show in a similar way that $f_+^{\tau\to\infty =f^{t_-}$ and $f_r^{\tau\to\infty =f^{r}$, so that the $\tau\to\infty$ is indeed the ballistic solution.}}
elements.sent-2411-601: We have numerically checked that the wall velocity is indeed a monotonic function of the proxy parameter $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-602: Therefore, the LTE and ballistic limits give the bounds on the wall velocity.. \bibliographystyle{utphys {.}}
elements.sent-2411-603: Here we describe the computation of the wall velocity in the ballistic limit for a number of toy models: the Standard Model with a light Higgs, and the Inert Doublet Model.
elements.sent-2411-604: In both cases, we already know what the LTE solution is, and, using WallGo, we can also compute the wall velocity in the presence of collision terms.
